summary: Charity Majors addresses how engineers at different career stages can drive change on their teams without formal authority. She argues that influence comes from good judgment, strategic relationship-building, and picking the right battles, offering tactical advice for junior engineers, new tech leads, and managers on how to introduce changes effectively.
key_points: Junior engineers should focus on learning fundamentals rather than trying to change established systems
New tech leads should understand existing systems before proposing changes and start with developer experience wins
Controversial ideas should be socialized one-on-one before presenting to a group to avoid triggering defensive reactions
Managers should aim to have others champion their ideas rather than pushing changes themselves
When overwhelmed by what to fix first, start with either observability or shortening the deploy pipeline
money_quotes: The actual best way to persuade people to listen to you is to make good decisions and display good judgment. Each of us has an implicit reputation score, which formal power can only overcome to an extent.
This is an apprenticeship industry. You're like a med student right now, who is just starting to do rounds under the supervision of an attending physician.

You are always going to get people's absolute worst reactions when you spring something on them in a group setting; any anxiety or dismay will be amplified tenfold. By letting them reflect and ask questions in private, you're giving time for their better selves to emerge.

Instead of having a shitty dynamic where engineers are attached to the old way of doing things and you are 'dragging them' into the newer ways against their will, you are inviting them to partner with you.
Put your glasses on before you start driving down the freeway, please.
key_insight: Driving change without authority requires building credibility through good judgment, socializing ideas one-on-one before group settings, and picking battles that deliver visible wins for the team.
